summary:
Overall UK risk remains Elevated. The picture continues to be shaped by external security shocks and domestic sensitivity to energy and cyber disruption: Russia’s long-range strike campaign against Ukraine remains high-intensity, and UK/NATO posture continues to emphasise deterrence and northern/Atlantic readiness.

Domestically, inflation remains above target and the UK remains exposed to renewed oil-price volatility (with knock-on effects for transport and household budgets) even as the current Ofgem energy price cap is lower for April–June. Operational disruption risk is also elevated due to confirmed London Underground strike action next week, while the cyber baseline remains elevated due to urgent patching requirements for widely deployed perimeter appliances.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Energy-price sensitivity remains a key UK risk channel: Brent is around $90/bbl, and CPI inflation remains at 3.0% (above target), even as the regulated energy price cap has eased for the current quarter.
drivers: Brent snapshot remains elevated (~90.38 USD/bbl), keeping fuel and inflation expectations sensitive to external shocks. https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/brent-crude-oil
ONS: CPI inflation was 3.0% in the 12 months to February 2026; core CPI was 3.2%. https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/inflationandpriceindices/bulletins/consumerpriceinflation/february2026

OBR (March 2026): real GDP growth forecast at 1.1% for 2026, with inflation expected to reach 2% target in late 2026 (baseline, but vulnerable to shocks). https://obr.uk/efo/economic-and-fiscal-outlook-march-2026/

Ofgem: energy price cap for 1 Apr–30 Jun 2026 is £1,641/year for a typical dual-fuel household paying by Direct Debit (down 6.6% vs prior quarter). https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: European security risk remains elevated due to the continuing high-intensity Russia–Ukraine war and ongoing long-range strikes, while UK domestic terrorism risk remains SUBSTANTIAL.
drivers: Reuters: Russia’s deadliest attack of the year so far hit Kyiv and other cities overnight on 16 April, with at least 17 killed across Ukraine. https://www.reuters.com/world/russian-missiles-hit-ukraines-kyiv-officials-report-injuries-fires-2026-04-16/

MOD/GOV.UK: UK Defence Secretary emphasised strengthening NATO, standing with Ukraine, and deploying the carrier group to the High North while leading NATO ‘Arctic Sentry’. https://www.gov.uk/government/speeches/defence-secretary-no9-speech-09-april-2026
MI5: UK national terrorism threat level is SUBSTANTIAL (an attack is likely). https://www.mi5.gov.uk/threats-and-advice/terrorism-threat-levels

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: No evidence reviewed indicates acute UK institutional instability; crisis-management posture is focused on monitoring external shocks and domestic economic impacts.
drivers: GOV.UK: COBR(M) meeting (23 Mar 2026) focused on domestic economic impacts of the Middle East situation; government discussed anti-profiteering measures and potential additional CMA powers. https://www.gov.uk/government/news/readout-cobrm-iran-economic-and-domestic-impacts-23-march-2026

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: Weather risk signals appear contained in the sources reviewed (no indication of widespread low-level snow; unsettled at times but not exceptional). Flood-alert national counts could not be reliably extracted in this run, so the category remains Guarded rather than being stepped down.
drivers: Met Office: no indication of widespread snow at low levels; unsettled at times through end of week with a trend toward more settled conditions from the weekend. https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/blog/2026/met-office-weather-headline-review-april-16
GOV.UK flooding service provides live alerts/warnings, but national counts could not be reliably extracted in this run. https://check-for-flooding.service.gov.uk/alerts-and-warnings

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: Disruption risk is elevated due to confirmed London Underground strike action next week, expected to affect all Underground lines and materially disrupt commuting.
drivers: Time Out: London Underground driver strike action scheduled for Apr 21–22 and Apr 23–24 (12pm to 11.59am), with significant disruption expected and all Underground lines affected. https://www.timeout.com/london/news/april-2026-tube-strikes-how-to-get-around-london-during-next-weeks-rmt-industrial-action-041626

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: Cyber/CNI risk remains elevated due to urgent patching requirements for widely deployed edge appliances (Citrix NetScaler), consistent with continued exposure to exploitation of internet-facing services.
drivers: NCSC: urges UK organisations to take immediate action on Citrix NetScaler vulnerabilities CVE-2026-3055 and CVE-2026-4368; install updated versions as soon as possible. https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/vulnerabilities-affecting-citrix-netscaler-adc-gateway

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics shows Brent crude oil at ~90.38 USD/bbl (page updated April 2026). https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/brent-crude-oil

indicators.name: UK CPI inflation (12 months to Feb 2026)
indicators.note: ONS: CPI rose by 3.0% in the 12 months to February 2026 (unchanged from January). https://www.ons.gov.uk/economy/inflationandpriceindices/bulletins/consumerpriceinflation/february2026

indicators.name: Ofgem energy price cap (typical dual-fuel, Direct Debit)
indicators.note: Ofgem: price cap for 1 Apr–30 Jun 2026 is £1,641/year (down 6.6% vs £1,758 for 1 Jan–31 Mar 2026). https://www.ofgem.gov.uk/information-consumers/energy-advice-households/energy-price-cap-explained

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat level
indicators.note: MI5: UK national terrorism threat level is SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely). https://www.mi5.gov.uk/threats-and-advice/terrorism-threat-levels

changes_since_last_update: Natural Hazards held at Guarded with explicit uncertainty because national flood alert/warning counts could not be reliably extracted in this run.
Reinforced the Military / Security rationale with Reuters reporting (16 April) on the deadliest strike wave of the year so far across Ukraine.
priority_watch_items: Energy spillovers: watch for renewed oil-price spikes feeding into UK fuel prices and inflation expectations.
European security: monitor for further large-scale Russia–Ukraine strike waves and any impacts on energy/commodity infrastructure.
Transport disruption: monitor confirmed London Underground strike impacts (21–24 April) and any wider industrial-action spillover.
Perimeter vulnerability exposure: confirm remediation/patching progress for Citrix NetScaler and watch for exploitation indicators.
Flooding/Weather: monitor EA flood updates and Met Office warnings for any rapid shift into a higher-impact event.
source_notes: This update prioritises UK official sources (ONS, Ofgem, MI5, NCSC, GOV.UK, Met Office) and Reuters for conflict context; Trading Economics is used for a Brent snapshot. https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/brent-crude-oil

The Environment Agency flood alerts page did not reliably return national count figures in automated extraction for this run; interpret Natural Hazards as a conservative holding assessment rather than a precise flood-warning count. https://check-for-flooding.service.gov.uk/alerts-and-warnings
